text: Welcome to the Startup Grind. Without any further ado, I'd like to welcome the CEO of INDEX, Sanjay Prathasana. Thank you, thank you, thank you, thank you, thank you. Thank you, thank you, sir. Thank you. Yes. That's the way that happens. Thank you, guys. Well, it's great just to have you here, so thanks for taking on short notice, by the way. Well, so let's just start off and kind of give everyone the 30,000-foot view of what you guys do. Okay. Because some people won't know that, and kind of definitely explain the vision. Like I asked that question, like where is it going to go? Give us the elevator pitch. Yeah, so we're building a product catalog in the cloud. Ultimately, we don't want any company who's interested in product information to build their own catalog. Just use us. Think of it like maps and location. You know, today we take for granted that every device, every piece of software uses map in the cloud. Use the map you probably got here using directions. Sure. I think that's going to happen for product information as well. And so that's what we're building. Okay? Yeah, yeah, yeah. Make sense? No, no. What's that? Can we do a quick question? No. So hold the questions to the end. So then we can get through some of this, and we'll have plenty of time at the end to answer or ask questions. So yes, no worries. So when you talk about that, where did the first of the idea of doing it came? I mean, you were at Microsoft for 19-ish years and fairly high level positions at Microsoft. Well, where did that idea come from? So after 19 years, I left Microsoft. Yeah. I didn't want to do anything for a year. So I ended up moving the family back to India where I grew up. And spent a year just meeting people, talking to people, playing with different ideas, going to conferences. Just kind of chilling, re-energizing, detoxing, whatever you want to call it. Just kind of doing something completely different. Yeah. Without a purpose. You know, I'm fortunate enough to be able to do that. Do that. Yeah, yeah. But that was important. Yeah. And it sprang out of that, it really sprang out of listening to other people. Yeah. And I'm sure you're sitting around talking with, you know, friends and saying, hey, we're, and this idea comes up, right? Did it, how has it changed from, let's just say, that weekend that you had the idea and started kind of culling it in your head? How has it changed to where it is today? You know, I think, at least I think it's a myth that you get an idea and somehow it grows. I think it's almost like a sculpture. You kind of, you start with a, you start with a stone and you keep removing things off it. He's chipping away. And I'll tell you how the idea kind of transformed. I'm paraphrasing, but I started off realizing that search engines don't work that well outside of the US and the UK. The PageRank algorithm basically gives you content in the US or the UK right up top. So you're in India and you're searching for stuff and, you know, things show up in the US. I'm going, that doesn't make sense. Yeah. So it started off as, hey, can you do a better search engine in countries outside of the US and the UK? Okay. Yes, you can. And there are very successful search engines, you know, in Korea, in Czech, in Russia. But pretty soon it became obvious that all of the, Google has all of the oxygen in the business model, the economics of the search engine. Yeah. Okay. So that's where it started. Then, you know, search engine for numbers. You know, that was just an idea. You know, what numbers are interesting? Places, latitude and longitude, time, and prices. Sure. These are all numbers. Yeah. You can do something interesting with a search engine for numbers, right? Yes, you can. And, you know, very soon I started to realize that prices had a lot of interesting things about them. I mean, they were economically valuable. Sure. Because you could predict inflation, you could price your product right. People would pay for that. But that was a limited problem. Right? Pretty soon I started to realize that products have lots and lots of different attributes. Not just prices. Hundreds, even thousands of attributes. Yeah. And that was a hard problem. To be able to collect every product on the planet and everything about every product, that's a hard problem. So that took about a year and a half to get from a local search engine to a product. Every product on the planet and everything about those products. Okay. So that's where you are now. You're now saying, okay, I'm going to categorize every product. Capture, organize, structure, you know, make available. Yeah. Yeah. Every product on the planet. So you're starting, because I know this because we just talked about it a few minutes ago, you're starting with the English language. That's right. But that's just a start. That's right. I mean, you can go to, like you say, French, German, Spanish, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. So, knowing what you know now, is this a 10-year project, 20-year project? Yes. Again, I'll use the analogy. Because it's a finite number. Although it's huge, it's somewhat finite. You know? What is finite? Sorry. Finite meaning it's a number that you can account. It's not like infinite. It's the opposite of infinite. Infinite is forever. There's no stop to it. That's right. So, if you look at location, it started, there were maps before 2000. I mean, TripIt and MapQuest. But the first serious company that I was aware of was a company in this area called Keyhole Systems. And they were bought by Google. And, you know, I worked with them at Microsoft in 2003, 2004. They were building maps so that businesses could use them to figure out locations for their stores. Right? And Google bought them and other companies. And, you know, that 15 years later, I mean, now we take it for granted. So, this is a 10-year project at least. We're about halfway through. We're finally starting to understand what it means. And if I'd known how hard it was, I wouldn't have started it. Yeah, yeah, yeah, exactly. That's true. That's true with most entrepreneurs, right? You don't realize how hard it is until you're two, three, four years into it. Like, holy crap, that's really hard. Right. Yeah. And the first three years, I mean, we changed our architecture pretty much every six months. Because we didn't realize how much data there would be. We didn't realize how much memory we'd need, you know, to do stuff. Sure. To do the analytics. How big the checks to AWS are writing. Yeah. How big, yeah, how much money we paid them. And, you know, the first four years, we raised money every year. Yeah. Because it was a much harder problem than I'd realized. And do you think, do you think right now, what you've, you know, what you've learned over the last, you know, several years, do you think you've now kind of got it figured out? No. No. No. Because you always, when you pick a hard problem, there's lots of things you could be working on. And we're still making bets on which part of the problem to spend the most resources on. Sure. And sometimes they don't work. They're not the right thing. Yeah, yeah. And, you know, what our customers wanted two years ago is very different than what our customers want now. Because things are moving fast outside as well. Yeah. Constantly. We do, we do strategy sessions every three months to figure out if we're still pointed in the right direction. Yeah. That, that actually is really smart. Just to that, that, that aiming piece that we sometimes as entrepreneurs, we get so far into the weeds that we don't necessarily look up and are we going in the right direction. So that's actually good advice just for anyone is that take the time to make sure you're going down the right path. Because like you say, every three months, which is great. That's, if you can be that discipline and like, hey, let's, let's check ourselves. Because some people go six months a year and realize, holy crap, we're not even going in the right direction now. Yeah. I think it's, that's, that's really important, I think. And in that three month check in that you do, like, is that a kind of all hands on deck thing? What is that? Executive team? Yeah. So I'm fortunate to have a pretty senior executive team. Our sales leader was in a public company. Our general counsel was in a public company. You know, several of my co-founders have started and sold their own company. Sure. You know, many of us are over 50. So there's a little experience. And so, you know, we, there's enough experience. And the, not the hardest thing, but the most important thing is for me to listen to them. Yeah. Yeah. There's enough good ideas and right ideas around. If I don't take the time to listen to them. Yeah. Right? Um, then I'd be making a huge mistake. Bad things happen. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. And, and the hardest thing is to listen to feedback, like, hey, we're off, you know, we're off track or we're doing the wrong things. Yeah. It's really hard to kind of take that in the moment. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. You kind of, kind of take a deep breath. Yeah. Before you react. Yeah. Um, and, and so what, what has Sanjay learned as an individual? Like, what are the things you've learned from 2010, and now it's 2017? What have you learned about you as an individual? Like, what, what is the thing that's been surprising for you? Like, what you learned is about, about you as an individual? What has been the surprise for, for Sanjay's, you know, call it whatever, but that being, Sanjay. Yeah. What have you learned about yourself? Good, good or bad? No, no, no. It's both. I mean, I'm not as quick to respond. I take more time to listen. Um, I'm not as smart as I thought I was. You're humbled. Um, yeah, I mean, I think a startup, I mean, the, the big difference between Microsoft, 19 years and five years here, is that the five years have built a better character. And Microsoft had built a better career. Sure, sure. Right? And so, they're very different things. Right? A career is when you learn to, when you learn how to deal with the system. Sure. A character is when you do, learn how to deal with yourself, your fears, your insecurities, your flaws. And so, very different. Yeah, yeah. That's very well put that, that character, because it is character building. I mean, most startups, and I think anyone that's in a startup or is going to be in a startup, that's one of the things you'll learn if you haven't learned already, is it's a huge character building exercise. All the demons that you put away in the closet are running around you at night. Absolutely. Yeah. So, now that you feel like you're a bit smarter as a human being, what things are you better at, let's rephrase that, what things are you worse at in your team guiding it down, guiding the rudder of the ship? What do you think you're worse at, that you know is a weakness? Like, here's something I just haven't been able to be good at yet. Well, I mean, I think I've lost a lot of that optimism and the kind of confidence. And so now I, you know, there's a certain optimism when you're 20 that really is an asset. I was going to say that. Okay. Is it the, you know, when you're 25 on the cricket team, you're a stud, right? And you just, you got it. Yeah. You know you can't be wrong. Exactly. Now I know I can be wrong. Yeah, exactly. And is it a weakness? Is it a strength? I mean, I wish I had that confidence of just putting your head down and just barreling straight through. Straight through. Yeah. I wish I could have that as well as the kind of character that's been built for the last five years. Yeah, the humbleness that you've figured out when to pull it out and when to put it in the back pocket. Yeah. So in your, you know, in your building of the team, which you've built actually a pretty amazing team. What has been the thing that you've learned from that? What's the thing that's actually allowed you to build such a really, it's a pretty damn strong team. I mean, it's for as small as you are, you're not a big company and you're not able to pay, you know, a quarter million dollar salaries. So how do you, you know, how do you do that and what kind of maybe some tricks that you've learned? Yeah. I think the, you attract senior people when you, when they know you're going to take advantage of their skills. Right. And you give them room to grow. Sure. And so we've got 10 very senior people who are here to learn. I mean, they may be over 50, most of them are over 40. Sure. But they're here because they learn from each other. Yeah. And this is a hard problem. So they know that they will learn from the problem itself. Yeah. And then I stay out of their way most of the time, which is what gives me the time to actually think about whether we're pointed in the right direction or not. Yeah. Right. So one of the important things to always test is whether you can trust them and whether you want to spend time with them outside of work. Yeah. Yeah. That's a, that's a, that's a theme that you hear all the smart guys say, smart guys, meaning men and women in that team building process is, do you want to be with them? Not during the office hours. Do you want to have a beer with them? Do you want to go to the soccer match? Do you want to do those things with them? That's the, I think that's a key, especially when you're a small company of six people. It's even super, it's even more critical. And remember we're in two countries separated by 13 and a half hours. Yeah. Right. And you can't keep track of what's going on in Chennai all the time. You just have to trust them. Yeah. Right. And if you can't do that. And that's true. It's not the distance. It's not the time difference. They could be sitting in the next office and you don't trust them. Yeah. It's just as bad. But you know, the distance forces you to trust people. And how have you, how have you learned to maybe test, I don't know how you say that. How do you know that you can trust, like how would you know that you could trust me versus Dave? I mean, how would you, how do you ferret that out? Well, I think. As a leader. I think there's a couple of ways to do it. Some people are comfortable with one way and not with the other. I try to have a conversation about, you know, values in a, in a, not, not in a specific sense, but in an abstract sense. So for example, one of my co-founders doesn't believe in titles, believes that every piece of information about everybody, including salaries need to be published. Now, I don't necessarily agree with that, but that is the same value system. Sure. Right. How far you go with transparency, there's always a discussion. Sure. If transparency is important to you, it's important to me. Sure. I know that we're going to get along. Yeah. Okay. Other things. He just ran 150 miles in Sahara Desert in seven days. Wow. Right. So, so here's, if you can do that. And I used to play, and I used to play, you know, sport at a pretty high level. Yeah. You know you have this ethic of showing up. Yeah. Yeah. Right. You don't have to worry about that. Yeah. So there's different ways to figure out whether you're compatible or not. Now, ideas are different. I mean, he and I have disagree on a lot of things. Yeah. But there's a foundation of trust. Yeah. And he's more, he's right more often than I am. Yeah. Yeah. I think that, again, that's one of those things that you learn as an entrepreneur is that, especially when you're hiring or partnering with someone at that early stage, you know, whether you're finding your co-founder. Because is there anyone here that's actually looking for a co-founder? Yeah. So two people. Looking for a co-founder. Three. So the process of that identification of who you can work with, is there any trick that you could tell these four people in the room that are going to go, is there something that you could say? Just, just, so I met this guy who runs the marathon, you know, ultra-marathons at a party. Sure. Another technical co-founder was recommended by my accountant. Okay. The third, a friend. So you just have to put yourself out there. Let everyone know that you're looking for them. Because you never know when they're going to be the right people. You know, you can't sit in an office and say, "Hey, I need a co-founder." Shoot emails to people. Did that work? No. You got to press some, you got to shake some people's hands. Yeah. So events like this. Yeah. This is where your likely co-founder is at. Yeah. Yeah. It's super important. And, you know, you got your team hired and you got a couple people now. You figured out what you're working on. You know, the problem is pretty clear. So what things did you, you know, what pushed you down the road of getting from, you know, the first, say, six months of the company to, say, two years down the road when you actually kind of, what I would say, were deeper and had the problem more clearly mapped out? How did you get through that process of, because like you said, when you start out, you think this is the problem. And many times, many, many times, the problem changes or the solution to the problem changes. It's never like, oh, I start this and I finish this. It's just perfect. It doesn't, I've never been in a company, ever worked like that. So how do you get some, what do you, what can you focus on to start to learn those things or, you know, the milestones? Like for you, what were the things that you got through that started maybe change the idea of how you solved or maybe change the idea of the problem or some things that you did? I think, you know, there's this notion of flow. And you can measure it in lots of different ways. You can talk to your engineers. You can talk to your salespeople and ask them if they're happy or not. And, you know, I think we take this notion of flow seriously. We use a program called, we use a piece of software called Tiny Pulse. Sure, just ask people. You know, are you happy? Yeah. And if they're not, and they'll tell you because it's anonymous, something's not going right. Yeah. So that's the technique that we use. Yeah. Which is, I mean, I've been in a couple of companies that we use the same product. Yeah. And, you know, flow is things are happening at velocity, at scale. And the engineers are working on the product. The salespeople are out meeting with customers. They know if it's not working. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Right? And we actually killed a product about a year ago. Really? Which was all our revenue based on feedback from our engineers and our salespeople. Really? Yeah. How did you do that? I mean, how do you, I mean, it's not actually probably not easy to answer just how do you do it. But how do you do that? Like, you're looking at, and I've heard this many times, like, you're looking at your revenue and your startups. It's not like you've got tons of revenue. Yeah. You've got this revenue stream. You're like, you know, we should kill that thing because it's not going to go where we want it to go. Because it was causing a lot, we were doing too many things that were causing dissatisfaction, whether it was manual stuff or, you know, custom stuff or whatever it was. It was just the wrong stuff. And the pain, you could feel the pain. Yeah. Yeah, yeah. And when it gets to that point, if you don't kill it, you kill the company. Yeah. Yeah, yeah. So it's the product or the company. And then the decision is actually fairly easy. Yeah. And did you, was it, was it, who kind of brought that to the forward, to the top of the bubbling water? Who brought it forward? Was that you or was it everybody saying, look what's going on here? I mean, once you're transparent enough that you're asking people for the bad stuff, everybody can see it. Yeah. And was it like an easy decision? Like the group was like, yeah, we've got to kill it. No. Because you can't just kill stuff without knowing what else you're going to do. It's going to work. Yeah, yeah. Exactly. So here you are, knowing that this is not right. Yeah. But what is? That's the hard part. You have to do both. Yeah. And that, if you don't have the solution, the new thing, then I think it would have been much harder to kill them. Yeah. But you already knew where to spend resources. It took us six months to figure it out, what was the right thing to do. Yeah. But yeah. Yeah, you already knew where the resources were or should go. And again, in this process of figuring things out, what things would you or should you be looking for as a leader? What things would you be looking for to identify when you have hit on the right one? Meaning the right answer, the right solution. What things as a leader should you be looking for to identify? Like that is the right one. Right. So I do still think of flow because it kind of abstracts a whole bunch of different things. But you also use business metrics. I mean, our average revenue per customer goes up. Yeah. The time to close goes down. Right. You know, you're raising quotas. You're raising, you know, your goals. Sure. All of that tells you that, yeah, it's going better. Yeah. It may not be perfect, but, you know, those are some metrics that you can use. Yeah. And how do you do that if you don't have those metrics? Like, let's say it's you and I just started a company, right? Right. We've went three months. We did this thing. We've realized it ain't the right thing. We're smart enough like, ah, it's like, right, right. So we've got to go over here. We think we've got the next thing. But we don't have enough data, really, to make that call yet. Right. How can, what can you, what, how would you do that? Right. So when you're starting a company, to know what's right, you've got to go pitch to people. Yeah. Humans. Real people. And most of them will tell you you're crazy, you're out of your mind. But there will be a few that really get it. And you got to, and you can see the energy. Right. Ultimately, that's what's going to keep you going. When you don't have business metrics, it's talking to people. Yeah. And, you know, my recommendation is in the first few years, raise money once a year. Because one, it will force you to think through what it is you're pitching. Sure. And two, you'll get rejected so often that you will, you will start pitching the right thing. Yeah, yeah. Because you'll learn. Because the fear of rejection is a great motivator. Exactly. Yeah. We did raise money the first three years, every year we raised money. Yeah. And then the fourth time was, you know, two years later. Yeah. So you're constantly testing your idea with people who are programmed to say no. Yeah. And while that's hard, it refines your thinking. Yeah. And that's the key there. I think the thing that's the super critical piece there is, one, talking to humans. Because you can't, you know, you can't give feedback without talking to somebody that's buying. That's actually writing a check for it. That's the difference. That's the difference. That's the difference. Are they going to write a check or are they just going to say it's a good idea? Because people say it's a good idea, it doesn't mean anything. Yeah. Don't talk to your friends. No, no. That's what I say. Only people, complete strangers are going to give you the best feedback. Yeah. And, you know, the first, when we were raising our seed round, I talked to 18 people and 17 of them ponied out. So that's good indication. Yeah. I mean, that's a pretty decent indication. Sure. Part of it is because they like you. No, and part of it is. But they still have a. Yeah, there is. But, you know, you're still asking them for money. Yeah. Yeah. They have to give you something. That's the key. Yeah. Yeah. Because I think one of the things that I've seen, you know, especially as I've gotten older, is just the people, they come in and, you know, they're asking questions. And my question is, how many people have you talked to? How many people have said they will write you a check? That is the question that many people have, the blind stare, like, oh crap, six people. That doesn't tell you anything. It must be 600 people. Like, that's the thing that you can't do. You can't do too much of talking to potential customers. It's literally impossible. Because the more you talk, the better feedback you get. Better details. In your personal kind of journey, what's been the hardest thing for you to do? I'm talking about from being the career guy, Microsoft, being the conscious, you know, real guy, you know, in the startup world. What's been the hardest thing for Sanjay to do? Just, is it, was it firing people at Microsoft? Was it climbing the ladder at Microsoft? Was it, what has been the hard thing for you? Initially, it was people not even bothering to respond. Right? I mean, rejection even before. Didn't even say no. Yeah, exactly. Didn't even ask a question. And they said no. That was hard because at Microsoft, everybody took my calls. Sure. Right. Yeah. It kind of gives you that, I want to say false sense of security. But there, but you have Microsoft behind you. Yeah. When you're a startup, you have. Yeah. But now, I mean, rejection now is no big deal. Sure. Right. I think the hardest thing to do is to control your emotions. It's all inside. The hardest problems are inside. Yeah. Controlling your emotions when you're hearing bad news, keeping your calm is the hardest thing to do. Yeah. And do you think it's harder for Sanjay in front of your leadership team? Or is it harder for you to do it in front of a customer? Where is it hardest for you? It's actually hardest to do when you're alone. When you're alone. Yeah. When you get an email and you're alone. Sure. It is really hard to kind of just take a moment and let it pass. Yeah. The last thing I have learned, this is where the character building comes in. Sure. Is to ignore it for a little while. Okay. I was going to say, ignore that you read it or just ignore the reaction? Ignore the reaction. Ignore the reaction. Ignore the reaction. Yeah. And come back. Don't react. Come back. And the whole thing is so much better. So much easier. So much easier. Yeah. Even better. The first thing that happens is, I mean, the moment you get this. Because I'm going to assume, not to interrupt, I'm going to assume that you're better now than you were when you left Microsoft. Better in what way? Better as a character. Yeah, absolutely. Yeah, that's what I'm saying. And your character has grown in a positive sense. In a positive sense. In a positive sense. Yeah. Communication. So, I will see the negatives of a particular situation. It is remarkable if you talk to somebody, two other people, any two other people, about the same situation, they will see it differently. And they're usually right. Say that again. Which is hard when your inner circle is like three people or four people. Or at least you feel like it is. Because founders are lonely at the top. We always hear they're lonely at the top.
